summaries.content: This video introduces nouns as naming words. Everything around us has a name, and that name is called a noun. Nouns can name people (girl, teacher), places (school, park), animals (dog, parrot), and things (pen, chair). Students learn to spot nouns in sentences by asking 'What is the name of this person, place, or thing?'
keyPoints: A noun is a naming word
Nouns name people, places, animals, and things
Every sentence has at least one noun
You can find nouns by asking who or what the sentence is about

summaries.content: This video explains the difference between common nouns and proper nouns. Common nouns are general names like boy, city, and river. Proper nouns are special names for particular people, places, or things and always start with a capital letter, like Aman, Delhi, and Ganga. Students also learn about collective nouns (group names) and abstract nouns (feelings and ideas).
keyPoints: Common nouns are general names and do not start with a capital letter
Proper nouns are special names and always begin with a capital letter
Collective nouns name groups like a flock, a team, a bunch
Abstract nouns name feelings and ideas like love, honesty, courage

summaries.content: This video teaches pronouns and why we use them. A pronoun takes the place of a noun so we do not repeat the same word. For example, instead of saying 'Priya went to the shop. Priya bought milk,' we say 'Priya went to the shop. She bought milk.' Common pronouns include I, you, he, she, it, we, and they.
keyPoints: A pronoun is a word used in place of a noun
Pronouns help us avoid repeating nouns
Common pronouns: I, you, he, she, it, we, they
The pronoun must match the noun it replaces

summaries.content: This video covers subject and object pronouns and how to use them correctly. Subject pronouns (I, he, she, we, they) do the action in a sentence. Object pronouns (me, him, her, us, them) receive the action. For example: 'She called him.' Here 'She' is the subject pronoun and 'him' is the object pronoun.
keyPoints: Subject pronouns do the action: I, you, he, she, it, we, they
Object pronouns receive the action: me, you, him, her, it, us, them
Choose the right pronoun based on its role in the sentence
Practice replacing nouns with pronouns in your own sentences
